architecture.summary: Gradient SAEs (g-SAEs) augment the traditional k-sparse autoencoder architecture by modifying the TopK activation selection function to incorporate gradients of input activations alongside activation values. The system learns a sparse, overcomplete decomposition of neural network internal activations, but selects which k elements to keep based on their downstream impact (via gradients) rather than activation magnitude alone. Information flows from network activations through gradient computation to TopK selection, then through the sparse reconstruction pathway.
trained_components: TopK selection mechanism with gradient weighting

biblio.title: Features that Make a Difference: Leveraging Gradients for Improved Dictionary Learning

claims.statement: g-SAEs produce reconstructions that are more faithful to original network performance when propagated through the network, compared to activation-value-only SAEs at a given sparsity level
g-SAEs learn latents that are on average more effective at steering models in arbitrary contexts
Traditional SAEs are biased towards neglecting features which are represented with small activation values but strongly influence model outputs
By incorporating gradients in TopK selection, g-SAEs account for the dual nature of neural network features as both representations (retrospectively) and actions (prospectively)
